does: This paper develops Themis-RM, a suite of multilingual code reward models trained to score code across multiple quality dimensions beyond functional correctness. The authors create Themis-CodeRewardBench, a benchmark evaluating reward models across five preference criteria (such as correctness, efficiency, readability, and maintainability) and eight programming languages, benchmarking over 50 existing models. They then construct Themis-CodePreference, a dataset of over 350,000 preference pairs for code quality, and use it to train flexible code reward models of varying sizes that can simultaneously assess multiple code attributes, enabling more nuanced evaluation than execution-based feedback alone.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on reward modeling for language model alignment and post-training, which has primarily focused on general text and mathematical reasoning. Existing code reward model research has been constrained to binary execution feedback and functional correctness, missing broader quality dimensions studied in software engineering. The paper responds to this limitation by adopting the multi-criteria evaluation framework used in broader RM research and combining it with multilingual code generation, where prior work has remained sparse. It builds on recent trends of scaling reward models for better policy gradient estimation and test-time scaling, while introducing systematic benchmarking to characterize RM performance across multiple languages and quality axes previously unexplored for code.
enables: This work enables more sophisticated reinforcement learning training of code generation models that can optimize for desirable attributes beyond mere correctness, including efficiency, readability, and maintainability. The large-scale preference dataset and benchmark infrastructure support future development of specialized reward models that better align code generation with real-world software engineering practices. The multilingual capability points toward globally equitable AI development, while the multi-criteria framework sets up research into compositional reward modeling and weighted preference learning for code. This foundation enables downstream work in test-time scaling for code generation and fine-tuning methods that balance competing objectives in software development.
